3 min read
Just now

--

A practical workflow for image-to-video tests, prompt consistency, and short vertical scenes.
Most failed AI character videos do not fail because the model is weak. They fail because the test changes too many variables at once.
A creator uploads one character image, changes the outfit, camera angle, lighting, location, expression, and action in the same prompt, then wonders why the output does not look like the same person anymore. The better workflow is slower at the beginning, but faster after the first good test.
Here is the simple process I use when testing short character videos.
Start With One Clean Reference
The reference image matters more than a long prompt.
A useful image-to-video reference should have a clear face, stable lighting, visible outfit shape, and a composition that matches the video you want to make. If the first image is blurry, cropped too tightly, or visually inconsistent, the video model has to guess too much.
For the first test, avoid changing identity details. Do not ask for a new hairstyle, a new face shape, a new outfit, and a new scene all at once. The first goal is not to make the final video. The first goal is to learn whether the character can stay recognizable for a few seconds.
Lock the Scene Before You Add Style
A short video prompt usually has four important parts:
1. The character reference
2. The action
3. The camera movement
4. The lighting and environment
In early tests, only one of these should change at a time.
For example, if you are testing a 9:16 vertical scene, keep the camera simple: “slow push-in” or “static portrait shot”. Keep the action simple too: “turns toward the camera” or “walks forward slowly”. This makes it easier to see whether the model is preserving the character or drifting away from the reference.
Use Short Tests Before Longer Scenes
Do not start with a complicated long sequence.
A 4–5 second test is enough to check identity, motion, lighting, and framing. If the character changes too much in that short test, a longer video will usually amplify the problem.
A good first test prompt can be very plain:
“Use the reference image as the same character. Create a short vertical video. The character stands in soft indoor lighting, turns slightly toward the camera, and keeps the same face, hairstyle, outfit, and overall visual identity. Slow camera movement. Natural motion.”
Once that works, add one new variable. Maybe a different camera move. Maybe a stronger background. Maybe a more expressive action. But keep the rest stable.
Separate Character Drift From Motion Problems
When a result looks wrong, it helps to ask what failed.
If the face changes, that is character drift. If the face is stable but the hands, walk, or body motion looks strange, that is a motion problem. If the character is stable but the scene feels flat, that is a styling or lighting problem.
Each problem needs a different fix.
Character drift usually needs a clearer reference image and a stricter identity prompt. Motion problems need simpler action. Styling problems need better scene direction, not more identity words.
This is why changing everything at once is so expensive: you cannot tell what caused the failure.
Build a Reusable Prompt Card
After a good test, save the working structure as a prompt card.
A simple prompt card can include:
- Reference image rules
- Fixed identity details
- Camera movement
- Scene lighting
- Action timeline
- Output format, such as 9:16 vertical video
- Things to avoid
Over time, these prompt cards become more valuable than one-off prompts. They turn video generation into a repeatable workflow instead of a guessing game.
Where Vipeak Fits
Vipeak AI is built around this kind of image-to-video workflow: start from a character reference, choose a visual direction, and test short video scenes without exposing every technical parameter at once.

I will keep documenting small workflow notes like this: reference consistency, short vertical tests, camera movement, and prompt cards for AI character video generation.
